skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Guides an AI assistant to check available skills before responding and to follow the selected skill workflow.
skill.summary: Enforces a skill-first workflow for AI assistants.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static findings for shell backticks and weak cryptography are false positives. The referenced lines contain Markdown prose and a Graphviz dot diagram, not executable code or cryptographic operations. The real concern is forceful agent-control language that could override normal assistant judgment, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Agent-Control Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant that it must check and use skills before any response, including clarifying questions. This is not confirmed malicious, but it may conflict with higher-priority instructions or user intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The forceful language is directly visible in the skill content. It is a behavioral-control risk, but there is no evidence of data theft, command execution, or malware.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Detection
low_findings.description: The detected block is a fenced Graphviz dot diagram that documents workflow states and transitions. It does not execute shell commands or invoke an interpreter.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full context shows a Markdown code fence labeled dot. No command syntax, shell execution, or user-controlled command input is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The detected lines contain ordinary skill description and priority guidance. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are prose and list items. They contain no cryptographic algorithms, function calls, or configuration values.

content.value_statement: AI assistants can skip relevant skills when work starts quickly. This skill creates a repeatable process for checking, selecting, and following skill instructions.
actual_capabilities: Directs the assistant to check available skills before responding.
Defines a flow for selecting and invoking an applicable skill.
Explains how to handle skills that include checklists.
Provides priority guidance for process skills and implementation skills.
Lists common rationalizations that lead assistants to skip skill checks.
limitations: Does not perform any task-specific work by itself.
Does not include scripts, integrations, or executable automation.
May be too broad for environments with strict tool-use policies.
Requires an assistant runtime that supports separate skills or skill tools.

use_cases.title: Standardize Assistant Intake
use_cases.description: Use a consistent first step before coding, writing, debugging, or research tasks.

use_cases.title: Reduce Missed Skill Usage
use_cases.description: Help assistants remember to check specialized skills before starting implementation work.
use_cases.target_user: Developers using Claude Code or Codex

use_cases.title: Document Skill Priority
use_cases.description: Give teams a simple rule for using process skills before domain or implementation skills.

prompt_templates.title: Start With Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Before answering my request, check whether any available skill applies. If one applies, state which skill you are using and why.

prompt_templates.title: Use Process First
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my task and decide whether a process skill should guide the approach before any implementation skill is used.

prompt_templates.title: Follow Skill Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: If the selected skill has a checklist, turn each checklist item into a tracked task before continuing.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Skill Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this conversation and identify where a skill should have been checked or used earlier. Explain the missed decision points.

output_examples.input: Fix this bug in my repository.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for debugging and repository skills before inspecting files, then follows the selected workflow.
The assistant considers process skills first, then applies relevant frontend or implementation skills during execution.

output_examples.input: Review whether our team uses skills consistently.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes missed skill checks, applicable skill categories, and recommended workflow changes.

best_practices: Use this skill as an intake rule, then let the selected task-specific skill guide the work.
Keep the available skill list current so the check is useful.
Review conflicts with system, developer, or organization-level instructions before publishing.
anti_patterns: Using this skill to override higher-priority safety or policy instructions.
Applying a skill when the task clearly does not match its purpose.
Treating the workflow diagram as executable automation.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands?
faq.answer: No. It is Markdown guidance and does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What is the main benefit?
faq.answer: It helps assistants check relevant skills before starting work.

faq.question: Can it complete tasks by itself?
faq.answer: No. It only defines a workflow for selecting and using other skills.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill uses forceful instructions that may conflict with normal assistant instruction priority.

faq.question: Is the Graphviz diagram executable?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation diagram inside a Markdown code fence.
